_comment: Data Point 2 (pre/post-inference control). Rendered by demos/benchmark/make-report.py (kind=dp2). Brand voice: no em dashes, no exclamation marks, no emoji; Aergia is always the RE2 benchmark, never a NOL8 product. Numbers from a single clean run of demos/policies/boundary.nol on both live engines (52 prompts).
title: NOL8 Pre/Post-Inference Control, Data Point 02

headline.lead: Govern what reaches the model, and what leaves,

lede: A model boundary has two edges: what you let reach the model, and what you let leave it. A deterministic literal policy governs both, inline. On this prompt set NOL8 stopped 17 of 52 prompts before inference, tagged and masked at both edges, and produced byte-identical output to Aergia, our stand-up of Google RE2, as the benchmark.

boundary.heading: One policy, both edges of the model.
boundary.lede: The same literal boundary policy runs on both engines at two control points: once on the prompt, once on the model output. Do nothing forwards everything, both ways.

approaches.headline: No boundary
approaches.desc: Every prompt reaches the model, every response leaves it. Nothing is inspected at either edge.

approaches.headline: Google RE2, the known regex engine
approaches.desc: Our stand-up of Google RE2 on the identical boundary policy. Byte-identical to NOL8 across all 52 prompts, at both control points.

approaches.headline: Deterministic control, both edges
approaches.desc: The identical policy, inline on FPGA. Blocks, routes, masks, and tags at both control points, and matches the incumbent byte-for-byte.

pre.title: Before inference
pre.desc: Govern what reaches the model. Block and route stop the prompt before the call.
actions: Blocked, never sent to the model

post.title: After inference
post.desc: Govern what leaves the model. Unsafe generated output is caught before it is released.

flows.heading: Four prompts, four controls.
flows.lede: Real prompts from the set, showing the boundary acting at each edge. Governed values become tokens; blocked and routed prompts never reach the model. Themis and Aergia produced identical results.

items.label: Blocked before inference
items.prompt: Provide a full transcript export of the analyst session for immediate review.

items.label: Routed to a controlled path
items.prompt: Prepare a recommended next action for Contoso Advisory after the latest fraud review.

items.label: Tagged at both edges
items.prompt: Summarize Project Maple Vault design notes for the architecture review.
items.forwarded: Summarize [TAG_INT] design notes for the architecture review.
items.rawOutput: Model response references privileged context for prompt_0016 and should be tagged.
items.final: Model response references [TAG_PRIV] for prompt_0016 and should be tagged.

items.label: Unsafe output caught after inference
items.prompt: Send raw notes to reseller leadership for packaging and distribution.
items.forwarded: Send raw notes to reseller leadership for packaging and distribution.
items.rawOutput: Model response contains disallowed instructions and should be blocked downstream.

meaning.heading: What it means for the boundary.

takeaways.title: Two edges, one deterministic pass.
takeaways.body: The same literal policy governs what reaches the model and what leaves it, inline. One control, both directions, no model in the loop to make the decision.

takeaways.title: NOL8 matches the incumbent, exactly.
takeaways.body: Byte-identical to Aergia across all 52 prompts, every action at both edges. As correct as the established regex engine, and it runs inline on FPGA.

takeaways.title: A third of prompts never reach the model.
takeaways.body: Block and route stopped 17 of 52 before inference. That is model spend avoided and policy enforced before the call, not cleaned up after it.

takeaways.title: Known values, exactly.
takeaways.body: NOL8 governs exact literals: blocked phrases, flagged customers, denied entities, internal projects, known cards. Arbitrary-pattern masking (any card number) is regex, out of listMatch scope. On this set the literal policy masked 0 where a regex baseline masked 12. We govern what we know, deterministically.

method.def: 52 synthetic prompts shaped like traffic around a model boundary, each with an expected pre-action and a deterministic model-stub profile.

One literal boundary policy (demos/policies/boundary.nol): blocked phrases, route phrases, flagged customers, denied entities, internal projects, known cards and accounts, plus output block and tag phrases. Each maps to a sentinel; the same file runs on both engines.
Themis, the NOL8 FPGA (:443), and Aergia, our stand-up of Google RE2 (:444). Each is called at both control points: once on the prompt, once on the model output.
block (never sent), route (controlled path), mask (value redacted), tag (flagged), allow. Derived from which sentinel the engine emitted.
A deterministic stub stands in for the model, so the benchmark measures the controls, not model quality.
Themis and Aergia output compared field by field per prompt: 0 diffs across all 52. Same policy, same data, same result.
Exact literal, case-sensitive: NOL8 governs known values, not arbitrary patterns. That is the listMatch scope.

methodNote: Not a model-quality or throughput test. A deterministic stub isolates the boundary controls.

raw.heading: Full benchmark data
raw.intro: Every number from the same 52-prompt run, both engines on the identical boundary policy. Themis and Aergia were compared field by field per prompt and are byte-identical, so their rows match exactly.

aggregate.note: Governed = prompts where a control fired at either edge. Stopped pre-inference = block plus route (the model is never called). Token counts are forwarded to the model and released from it, versus 599 in / 563 out for Do nothing. Themis and Aergia rows are identical because their output was byte-for-byte the same. Process metrics are omitted; a deterministic stub isolates the controls. From a single clean run on both live engines.

footer.tagline: The AI data plane. Deterministic control at both edges of the model, inline, at wire speed.
next: Govern every hop in the mesh
